**Willesden** ([/ˈwɪlzdən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is an area of north-west [London](/wiki/London "London"), situated 5 miles (8 km) west north-west of [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross"). It is historically a parish in the county of [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex")[[1]](#cite_note-1) that was incorporated as the [Municipal Borough of Willesden](/wiki/Municipal_Borough_of_Willesden "Municipal Borough of Willesden") in 1933; it has formed part of the [London Borough of Brent](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Brent "London Borough of Brent") in [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") since 1965.[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Dollis Hill](/wiki/Dollis_Hill "Dollis Hill") is also sometimes referred to as being part of Willesden.

With its close proximity to affluent neighbourhoods [Brondesbury Park](/wiki/Brondesbury_Park "Brondesbury Park"), [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park,_London "Queen's Park, London") and [Kensal Rise](/wiki/Kensal_Rise "Kensal Rise"), the area surrounding [Willesden Green station](/wiki/Willesden_Green_station "Willesden Green station") has seen increased gentrification in the past several years, with rapidly rising property prices. *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* described Willesden Green as one of London's "new middle class" areas.[[3]](#cite_note-brignall1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-mcghie1-4) The area has a population of 44,295, as of [2021](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census"), including the [Willesden Green](/wiki/Willesden_Green_(ward) "Willesden Green (ward)"), [Dollis Hill](/wiki/Dollis_Hill "Dollis Hill") and Dudden Hill wards. Willesden Green has one of the city's highest [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") populations, and is also strongly associated with [Afro-Caribbeans](/wiki/British_Afro-Caribbean_community "British Afro-Caribbean community") and [Latin Americans](/wiki/Latin_American "Latin American").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Willesden is mostly in the [NW10](/wiki/NW10 "NW10") postcode district, but part of it is in the [NW2](/wiki/NW_postcode_area "NW postcode area") postcode district.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name derives from the Anglo-Saxon *Willesdune*, meaning the Hill of the Spring,[[6]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-6) and a Manor (landholding) bearing this name was recorded in 939 AD. The [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") of 1086 records the manor as *Wellesdone*.[[6]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-6) However, on 19th century maps of the town such as those from the 'Ordnance Survey First Series', the town is shown as Wilsdon.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The current spelling was adopted by the [London and Birmingham Railway](/wiki/London_and_Birmingham_Railway "London and Birmingham Railway") in 1844, when they opened a local station.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/St._Marys_Church%2C_Neasden_Lane%2C_NW10_%28geograph_2822687%29.jpg/250px-St._Marys_Church%2C_Neasden_Lane%2C_NW10_%28geograph_2822687%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Marys_Church,_Neasden_Lane,_NW10_(geograph_2822687).jpg)

Church of St. Mary on Neasden Lane, Willesden

Willesden became a [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish#ancient_parish "Civil parish") in the medieval period. From the 14th to 16th centuries, the town was a place of pilgrimage due to the presence of two ancient statues of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") at the Church of St Mary. One of these statues is thought to have been a [Black Madonna](/wiki/Black_Madonna "Black Madonna"), venerated as [Our Lady of Willesden](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Willesden "Our Lady of Willesden"), which was insulted by the [Lollards](/wiki/Lollards "Lollards"), taken to Thomas Cromwell's house and burnt in 1538 on a large bonfire of "notable images" including those of [Our Lady of Walsingham](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Walsingham "Our Lady of Walsingham"), Our Lady of Worcester, and [Our Lady of Ipswich](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Ipswich "Our Lady of Ipswich"). There was also a "holy well" which was thought to possess miraculous qualities, particularly for blindness and other eye disorders.
Much of the district supplied apples, pears and vegetables to the city of London for many years from the early years of the industrial revolution.

### Industrial history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Industrial history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/MHV_Iris_15_hp_1912.jpg/250px-MHV_Iris_15_hp_1912.jpg)](/wiki/File:MHV_Iris_15_hp_1912.jpg)

Iris 15 HP (1912)

The [Iris](/wiki/Iris_(car) "Iris (car)") was a British car brand that was manufactured from 1906 by Legros & Knowles Ltd in Willesden. Lucien Alphonse Legros (1866–1933), son of the artist Alphonse Legros, and [Guy Knowles](/wiki/Guy_Knowles "Guy Knowles"), scion of a wealthy and artistic family, founded Legros & Knowles Ltd in Cumberland Park, Willesden Junction, in 1904 to build and repair vehicles.[[9]](#cite_note-Grace's_Legros-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Grace's_L&K-10)[[11]](#cite_note-MotorSport_1960-11)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Willesdengreenstation.jpg/250px-Willesdengreenstation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willesdengreenstation.jpg)

Willesden Green station opened in 1879

The parish of Willesden remained predominantly rural up until 1875, when its population was 18,500. It included the villages and hamlets of [Brondesbury](/wiki/Brondesbury "Brondesbury"), [Dollis Hill](/wiki/Dollis_Hill "Dollis Hill"), Dudden Hill, [Harlesden](/wiki/Harlesden "Harlesden"), [Kilburn](/wiki/Kilburn,_London "Kilburn, London"), [Mapesbury](/wiki/Mapesbury "Mapesbury"), Oxgate and [Stonebridge](/wiki/Stonebridge,_London "Stonebridge, London").[[12]](#cite_note-12) However, this changed with the opening of the Metropolitan Railway (later the [Metropolitan line](/wiki/Metropolitan_line "Metropolitan line")) station of Willesden Green on 24 November 1879. By 1906 the population had grown to 140,000, a phenomenon of rapid growth that was to be repeated in the 1920s in neighbouring areas such as [Harrow](/wiki/Harrow,_London "Harrow, London"). The Metropolitan line service was withdrawn in 1940, when the station was served by the Bakerloo line,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and later the [Jubilee line](/wiki/Jubilee_line "Jubilee line").

The [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") caused Willesden to change from a predominantly [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") suburb to a [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") part of London. After the war, Willesden grew rapidly as many factories opened up with numerous flats and terraced houses. The local council encouraged building to prevent large unemployment and decline.
To the present day, Willesden has been shaped by the patterns of migration which marks it out as one of the most diverse areas in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). [City of London Corporation](/wiki/City_of_London_Corporation "City of London Corporation") records show that the first black person recorded in Brent was Sarah Eco, who was christened in [St. Mary's Church](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Church,_Willesden "St Mary's Church, Willesden") in Willesden on 15 September 1723.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The [1901 United Kingdom census](/wiki/1901_United_Kingdom_census "1901 United Kingdom census") recorded that 42% of the population was born in London. In 1923, the specialist coach builder [Freestone and Webb](/wiki/Freestone_and_Webb "Freestone and Webb") established their base in Willesden, producing bespoke cars on [Rolls-Royce](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Limited "Rolls-Royce Limited") and Bentley chassis until 1956.

Willesden became a municipal borough in 1933, and it is at this time that the area became predominantly working class. A small [Irish](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora") community had formed in Willesden by this time, which grew rapidly during the period of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). A small Jewish community of refugees from Europe also formed during the war, with 3.5% of the population in 1951 born in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") or [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria").
During the war, Willesden suffered large bombing damage due to the heavy concentration of manufacturing industry, such as munition factories, the location of 'Smiths Instruments" (Used defensive aircraft instrumentation). Mulliner-Park Ward (Coach builders to Rolls-Royce and Bentley, hand built cars). Power Station location, canal and major railway locomotive overhaul facilities located in the area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Willesden_Jewish_Cemetery_prayer_hall.jpg/250px-Willesden_Jewish_Cemetery_prayer_hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willesden_Jewish_Cemetery_prayer_hall.jpg)

The prayer hall of [Willesden Jewish Cemetery](/wiki/Willesden_Jewish_Cemetery "Willesden Jewish Cemetery")

The period from 1960 saw migrants settling from the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") and the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). Additionally, from 1963 it was the site of the [Kuo Yuan](/wiki/Kuo_Yuan "Kuo Yuan"), the first Chinese restaurant to serve Pekinese dishes in Britain.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Since the 1960s, Willesden has been popular with young working holidaymakers from [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), although this popularity has declined somewhat in favour of other areas since about 2003.

Willesden went into a period of decline during the 1970s and 1980s as much of the housing was inadequate due to overcrowding as industry was mixed with housing. The whole of central Willesden (bar the area by the Willesden Green station) was earmarked for redevelopment; however, this did not come to fruition. In the late 1980s, traders were given money to revamp the High Street to prevent shops closing.

The area surrounding [Willesden Green station](/wiki/Willesden_Green_station "Willesden Green station") has become more middle-class and gentrified with marked property price rises in 2014 and 2015.[[3]](#cite_note-brignall1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-mcghie1-4)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Politics")]

The [Willesden Green ward](/wiki/Willesden_Green_(ward) "Willesden Green (ward)") is represented on [Brent Council](/wiki/Brent_Council "Brent Council") by three [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") [councillors](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor"), Janice Long, Saqlain Choudry, and Tom Miller.

Willesden forms part of the [Brent East](/wiki/Brent_East "Brent East") parliamentary constituency and is home to the local [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") MP [Dawn Butler](/wiki/Dawn_Butler "Dawn Butler").

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demographics")]

According to the 2011 census, the Willesden Green ward had a population of 15,587. Ethnically, 22% of the population was Other White, followed by 20% White British, 8.2% Other Asian, 8.1% Black African and 7.1% Black Caribbean.[[15]](#cite_note-auto-15) 52.7% were [BAME](/wiki/BAME "BAME").[[16]](#cite_note-16) The most spoken foreign language is Portuguese. 2,621 of the tenure households were privately rented; 1,625 were socially rented; 1,540 were owned.[[15]](#cite_note-auto-15)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Rail and tube

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Rail and tube")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Willesden_Green_Tube_Station.JPG/330px-Willesden_Green_Tube_Station.JPG)](/wiki/File:Willesden_Green_Tube_Station.JPG)

Willesden Green station

The area is served by three stations:

* [Dollis Hill tube station](/wiki/Dollis_Hill_tube_station "Dollis Hill tube station") (Jubilee line)
* [Willesden Green tube station](/wiki/Willesden_Green_tube_station "Willesden Green tube station") (Jubilee line)
* [Willesden Junction station](/wiki/Willesden_Junction_station "Willesden Junction station") (Bakerloo line and London Overground).

Three [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground") lines pass through Willesden:[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)

* [Jubilee line](/wiki/Jubilee_line "Jubilee line"): this connects the area directly to [Stanmore](/wiki/Stanmore_tube_station "Stanmore tube station") via [Wembley Park](/wiki/Wembley_Park_tube_station "Wembley Park tube station") northbound, and to [Central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London") southbound. Key southbound destinations include [Baker Street](/wiki/Baker_Street_tube_station "Baker Street tube station"), [Bond Street](/wiki/Bond_Street_tube_station "Bond Street tube station"), [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster_tube_station "Westminster tube station"), [Waterloo](/wiki/London_Waterloo_station "London Waterloo station") and [Canary Wharf](/wiki/Canary_Wharf_tube_station "Canary Wharf tube station"). Most southbound services terminate at [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford_station "Stratford station").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

* [Bakerloo line](/wiki/Bakerloo_line "Bakerloo line"): northbound trains from Willesden Junction terminate at nearby [Stonebridge Park](/wiki/Stonebridge_Park_station "Stonebridge Park station"), with some continuing towards [Wembley Central](/wiki/Wembley_Central_station "Wembley Central station") and [Harrow & Wealdstone](/wiki/Harrow_%26_Wealdstone "Harrow & Wealdstone"). Southbound services also pass through Central London, with trains to [Paddington](/wiki/London_Paddington_station "London Paddington station"), [Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone_station "Marylebone station"), Baker Street, [Oxford Circus](/wiki/Oxford_Circus_tube_station "Oxford Circus tube station"), Waterloo and [Elephant & Castle](/wiki/Elephant_%26_Castle_tube_station "Elephant & Castle tube station").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

* [Metropolitan line](/wiki/Metropolitan_line "Metropolitan line"): trains pass through Willesden Green and Dollis Hill, but do not stop; this has not always been the case. Willesden Green station was opened by the [Metropolitan Railway](/wiki/Metropolitan_Railway "Metropolitan Railway") in 1879; the area owes much of its development to it and to [Metro-land](/wiki/Metro-land "Metro-land"). Today, passengers from Willesden can access the Metropolitan line by using the Jubilee line and changing at either [Wembley Central](/wiki/Wembley_Central_railway_station "Wembley Central railway station") to the north or [Finchley Road](/wiki/Finchley_Road_tube_station "Finchley Road tube station") to the south.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Metro-land-map.jpg/330px-Metro-land-map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metro-land-map.jpg)

Map of Metro-land, showing the Metropolitan Railway passing through Willesden (1924)

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Buses")]

A large bus garage was built in 1902 and so many bus routes start or run through the town. [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") visited it during her Golden Jubilee celebrations.

[London Buses](/wiki/London_Buses "London Buses") routes serving Willesden are: [6](/wiki/London_Buses_route_6 "London Buses route 6"), [52](/wiki/London_Buses_route_52 "London Buses route 52"), [98](/wiki/London_Buses_route_98 "London Buses route 98"), 206, 226, 260, 266, 297, 460 and N98.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Willesden&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Cycling")]

To the north of Willesden, [Quietway 3](/wiki/List_of_cycle_routes_in_London "List of cycle routes in London") runs unbroken between [Gladstone Park](/wiki/Gladstone_Park,_London "Gladstone Park, London") and [Shoot Up Hill](/wiki/A5_road_(Great_Britain) "A5 road (Great Britain)") on quiet, residential streets. The route is coordinated by [Transport for London](/wiki/Transport_for_London "Transport for London") (TfL) and is planned to extend eastbound into [West Hampstead](/wiki/West_Hampstead "West Hampstead") towards [Regent's Park](/wiki/Regent%27s_Park "Regent's Park").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

A direct, traffic-free cycle route runs to the south of Willesden along the [Grand Union Canal](/wiki/Grand_Union_Canal "Grand Union Canal") [towpath](/wiki/Towpath "Towpath"). Cyclists [share the route](/wiki/Shared_use_path "Shared use path") with pedestrians, but the towpath provides cyclists with an unbroken, traffic-free connection to [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington "Paddington"). From Paddington, cyclists can access further Central London destinations using traffic-free [Cycle Superhighway 3](/wiki/Cycle_Superhighway_3 "Cycle Superhighway 3").
